our_argument_summary: Our review states the 2.26× longitudinal-to-latitudinal drift ratio is arithmetic from published NOAA pole position data, not a dome-geometry derivation. WIN-007 uses the same NOAA trajectory dataset; WIN-043 extracts a different quantity (velocity component ratio) from those same observations. The ratio is purely descriptive. Verdict: Std Model Explains.

relevant_files: monitor/baseline/01-home.txt — line 48: 'NMP longitudinal dominance 2.26×' listed as prospective confirmed
raw-text/03-predictions.txt — W019: 'NMP drift direction this week: Poleward dominates lateral (PENDING)' — related weekly test
raw-text/02-wins.txt — PROS-002 references NP.xy dataset and WIN-007
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitor.py available in current raw-text directory. Per Cycle 1 review and WIN-007 analysis, Domain #11 (NMP monitoring) fetches NOAA NP.xy data and checks whether current longitude > 130°E. No domain computes the 2.26× longitudinal/latitudinal velocity ratio. The ratio appears only as a text-level claim on the dome homepage and is not validated by any automated script.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Neither — the 2.26× ratio has no monitoring domain whatsoever. Domain #11 validates only the pole's longitude, not the drift direction decomposition. The ratio is an unmonitored assertion.
data_sources_used: NOAA NP.xy (pole position time series — same source as WIN-006, WIN-007, WIN-036)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The 2.26× ratio is stated without specifying: (1) the time window for velocity computation, (2) whether instantaneous or average velocities are used, (3) the coordinate system (geographic vs. geomagnetic). This ambiguity allows retroactive recalculation if the ratio changes due to the NMP's ongoing deceleration since ~2019.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: No discrepancies found. Our review correctly states the ratio is arithmetic from NOAA data with no dome-geometry derivation.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitor.py available for direct inspection. Cycle 1 review confirmed Domain #11 is the NMP domain, but it only checks pole longitude > 130°E — it does not decompose drift into directional components or compute any ratio. No other domain addresses velocity component ratios. The 2.26× value is unmonitored text.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The NMP's trajectory and directional asymmetry are fully explained by standard core-mantle boundary flux dynamics. Livermore et al. (2020, Nature Geoscience) explain the predominantly longitudinal drift as competition between Canadian and Siberian flux lobes. The dome renames the cause ('aetheric vortex threshold crossing') without changing the predicted value or providing an alternative derivation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: NOAA has published NP.xy pole position data with modern high-cadence tracking since the 1900s. The 2.26× ratio can be computed from this publicly available dataset. The dome model was created well after these measurements were published. The dome's own W019 weekly test ('NMP drift direction this week: Poleward dominates lateral') was registered 2026-03-12 and remained PENDING as of the last snapshot, suggesting the directional claim was not formally pre-registered with numerical precision.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula on the dome site derives the 2.26× ratio from dome geometry. The dome's H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) is axially symmetric — it depends only on radial distance and has no mechanism for predicting the ratio of longitudinal to latitudinal drift. This point is now reinforced by the Axial Symmetry Paradox section in sections.json, which explicitly names WIN-043 among WINs making longitude-dependent claims from a radially symmetric model.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence still does not attempt to verify the 2.26× value. ISS-223 was closed as 'fixed' but the current text only says the ratio is 'purely descriptive — computed by dividing published velocity components.' A rough check reveals a problem: pole moved from ~(82.7°N, 245.6°E) in 1990 to ~(86.5°N, 139.3°E) in 2025. Total longitude change ~106° (shortest path) vs latitude change ~3.8° — that's a ratio of ~28×, not 2.26×. The dome must be computing over a specific shorter interval or using instantaneous velocity components at a particular epoch. Without knowing the dome's methodology, 2.26× is unverifiable. We should note this explicitly: 'The dome does not specify the time window or velocity method used to compute 2.26×; naïve computation over the full 1990–2025 trajectory yields a much larger ratio, suggesting the value is epoch-specific and will change as the NMP decelerates.'
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_evidence or detail_extra: 'The dome does not specify how the 2.26× ratio is computed — time window, velocity method (instantaneous vs. average), and coordinate system are all unspecified. Computing total longitudinal vs. latitudinal displacement from NOAA NP.xy over 1990–2025 yields a ratio far larger than 2.26×, suggesting the value is epoch-specific. An epoch-dependent ratio is a snapshot, not a physical prediction — it will change as the NMP continues to decelerate (documented since ~2019, Chulliat et al. 2020).'

holes_found.description: The NOAA link (https://www.noaa.gov/) is correct but non-specific — it links to NOAA's homepage rather than the NP.xy dataset or WMM page. The actual data source is more precisely https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/data/poles/NP.xy. Using the general NOAA URL is not wrong but is less rigorous than a direct dataset citation.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider linking to the specific NOAA dataset URL (https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/data/poles/NP.xy) or the WMM page, at least in one WIN in the NMP cluster (WIN-006/007/036/043). Not a high priority — the generic NOAA link is acceptable.

holes_found.description: The finding text says 'standard core-mantle boundary dynamics predicts the same drift anisotropy without a dome' — this is accurate but slightly oversells what standard models 'predict.' Standard models (IGRF/WMM) describe the current field and its secular variation; they don't independently 'predict' the 2.26× ratio either. What's more precise is that standard geophysics explains WHY the ratio exists (flux lobe competition) and the ratio falls out naturally from these models' descriptions of the observed field, whereas the dome has no mechanism at all.
holes_found.recommendation: Minor wording adjustment for precision: 'standard core-mantle boundary dynamics explains the same drift anisotropy' rather than 'predicts.' Or keep as-is — the distinction is subtle and the argument works either way.

stronger_arguments.description: The Axial Symmetry Paradox section (from EXP-012) now exists in sections.json and explicitly names WIN-043 as one of 12+ WINs making longitude-dependent claims from a radially symmetric model. WIN-043's individual detail text should cross-reference this systemic argument. Adding a sentence like 'See Section 4.5: the dome's axially symmetric field equation H(r) cannot produce longitude-dependent predictions, of which this directional ratio is one' would connect the per-WIN evidence to the structural critique.

The dome's own W019 weekly test ('NMP drift direction this week: Poleward dominates lateral') remained PENDING as of the latest snapshot. This is noteworthy: the dome registered a directional prediction for the NMP but hasn't confirmed it. Meanwhile WIN-043 claims the 2.26× ratio as 'CONFIRMED.' If the dome can't confirm its own weekly direction test, the basis for claiming the ratio as a confirmed prediction is undermined.

kernel_of_truth.description: The NMP does exhibit pronounced longitudinal dominance in its recent trajectory. The pole has moved dramatically eastward (from ~245°E to ~139°E, crossing the prime meridian) while gaining only ~4° of latitude since 1990. This is a real and geophysically significant feature. The dome model correctly identifies that pole drift is directionally biased — a genuine observation that many laypeople might not appreciate.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Three problems persist: (1) The ratio is computed from publicly available NOAA data — this is retrodiction, not prediction. (2) The dome's axially symmetric geometry (H(r) depends only on r) structurally cannot predict longitude-dependent quantities. (3) Standard geophysics explains the directional asymmetry via Siberian/Canadian flux lobe competition (Livermore 2020). The dome adds no mechanism, derivation, or predictive power beyond labeling the observation.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 review of WIN-043. Comparing against Cycle 1 (reviewed 2026-04-06T23:55:00Z):

**What Changed Since Cycle 1:**
All four Cycle 1 issues (ISS-222 through ISS-225) plus ISS-443 were closed as 'fixed.' The text has been improved:
- No longer calls WIN-043 a 'duplicate of WIN-007' — now correctly says 'WIN-007 already confirms the pole's position and acceleration from the same NOAA trajectory dataset; WIN-043 extracts a different arithmetic quantity.'
- The 'Non-discriminating' framing in the verdict text is stronger than the Cycle 1 version.
- Verdict remained 'Std Model Explains' as recommended.

**What Hasn't Changed:**
- The 2.26× value is still not verified or questioned. ISS-223 was closed but the fix only added wording about the ratio being 'purely descriptive,' not an actual verification attempt. The naïve full-trajectory computation yields a ratio far larger than 2.26×, suggesting the dome's value is epoch-specific. This ambiguity should be noted.
- The NOAA link still points to the generic homepage rather than the specific NP.xy dataset.

**Axial Symmetry Integration:**
The Axial Symmetry Paradox section now exists in sections.json and names WIN-043 explicitly. This is excellent — the strongest structural argument against WIN-043 is now in the review. However, WIN-043's individual detail text doesn't cross-reference it. A one-sentence cross-reference would connect the per-WIN argument to the systemic critique.

**Code Analysis Tags:**
All tags confirmed unchanged and still correct:
- monitoring: none — no domain computes the 2.26× ratio
- relabels_standard: true — standard flux lobe dynamics explains the directional asymmetry
- post_hoc: true — NOAA data predates the dome model
- derives_from_dome: false — no dome-geometry derivation exists; axial symmetry precludes one

**Cycle 2 Assessment:**
The text is materially improved from Cycle 1. The remaining issues are moderate-to-minor: the unverified 2.26× value (which may reveal the ratio is epoch-specific), non-specific NOAA citation, and a subtle overselling of what standard models 'predict' vs 'explain.' The verdict and core argument are sound. Overall confidence in the entry's quality is 0.92.

citations_verified: https://www.noaa.gov/ — NOAA main page, linked in detail_evidence. URL correct and active.
citations_unchecked: Livermore et al. 2020, Nature Geoscience — Referenced in stronger_arguments and code_analysis_tags. Already verified in Cycle 1 WIN-007 and WIN-043 reviews. DOI resolves correctly.
Chulliat et al. 2020, Earth, Planets and Space 72:27 — Referenced in recommendations for NMP deceleration context. Not verified via direct DOI resolution this session.
NOAA NP.xy dataset (https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/data/poles/NP.xy) — Referenced as the actual data source. Verified accessible in prior reviews.
